_meta.title: Memory Allocators in Production — jemalloc, mimalloc

questions.question: A bump allocator cannot reuse freed individual allocations. What lifetime pattern makes it a good choice despite this limitation?
options: Objects with independent lifetimes freed at arbitrary times
Arena-style lifetimes where all objects are freed together
Reference-counted objects with unpredictable deallocation order
Long-lived singleton objects that persist for the process lifetime
questions.explanation: Bump allocators excel in arena-style patterns where you allocate forward and free the entire region at once, such as parse trees, game frames, or temp allocators within a function.

questions.question: A program requests 17 bytes, but the allocator returns a 32-byte block. What type of fragmentation does the 15 wasted bytes represent?
options: External fragmentation — free memory is split into small holes
Internal fragmentation — wasted space inside the allocated block
Slab fragmentation — inefficient slab size class selection
Cache fragmentation — data spans multiple cache lines
questions.explanation: Internal fragmentation occurs when the allocator rounds up to a size class (17 → 32 bytes), wasting space inside the block. External fragmentation is about free holes between blocks.

questions.question: In jemalloc, what is the primary purpose of tcache (thread cache)?
options: To reduce internal fragmentation by storing exact-size allocations
To provide a lock-free fast path for allocation and free operations per thread
To coordinate arena assignment across multiple threads
To compress memory pages and reduce RSS usage
questions.explanation: tcache is a per-thread LIFO of free slots per size class. Allocation from tcache is lock-free and O(1), avoiding the arena lock. Most allocations hit the tcache fast path.

questions.question: mimalloc uses 'free list sharding' where each page has both a local free list and a thread-free list. When Thread B frees an object allocated by Thread A, what happens?
options: Thread B acquires the arena lock and returns the object to Thread A's arena
Thread B atomically pushes onto the page's thread-free list; Thread A swaps it in later
Thread B signals Thread A via a condition variable to reclaim the object
Thread B immediately returns the object to the OS via madvise
questions.explanation: mimalloc's key design: foreign frees use an atomic push to the page's thread-free list. When the owning thread's local free list is empty, it swaps local_free and thread_free in a single atomic operation, batch-reclaiming all foreign frees.

questions.question: When should you use a custom allocator instead of the system malloc?
options: When your program is I/O-bound and you want to reduce CPU usage
When you have measured lock contention or fragmentation in the system allocator and have a known allocation pattern
When you want to avoid learning how malloc works internally
When your program allocates fewer than 100 objects per second
questions.explanation: Custom allocators are justified when profiling shows a real problem (contention, fragmentation) and your allocation pattern is known and regular (object pools, arenas, fixed sizes). Not when your program is I/O-bound or rarely allocates.

questions.question: You benchmark an allocator and find p99 allocation latency spikes at 50μs. What is the most likely cause?
options: Internal fragmentation causing size-class mismatches
Thread contention on a global or arena lock during slow-path allocation
The allocator's metadata consuming too much memory
The program is not calling malloc enough times per second
questions.explanation: P99 latency spikes in allocation almost always indicate lock contention — when the fast path (tcache/local free list) is empty and multiple threads compete for the arena lock or page lock simultaneously.
